note_data.title: Acids and bases
keyPoints: Compound - 2 or more elements chemically joined together - Contains Covalent or Ionic Bonds
Mixtures - 2 or more substances which are not chemically joined together - Can consist of Elements, Compounds, or Elements + Compounds
Pure Substances have: Fixed composition, Fixed Mp/Bp, Produces only 1 spot on chromatogram, All molecules same (equal)
Mixtures have: Variable Composition, Variable Bp/Mp, 2 or more spots on Chromatogram, 2 or more diff. molecules
Solid & Liquid solution → Crystallization (Obtaining solid) – Solute
Solid & Liquid solution → Distillation (Obtaining Liquid) – Solvent
Mixture of Organic Compounds → Chromatography (Eg. Colour Dyes)

definitions.definition: no more solid can dissolve in it

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the exact definition that matches the mark scheme
Give detailed observations including colour changes and pH values

modelAnswer.question: Explain why sodium hydroxide is described as an alkali but copper oxide is described as a base. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Sodium hydroxide is an alkali because it is soluble in water and produces hydroxide ions (OH-) when dissolved. Copper oxide is a base because it reacts with acids to form salt and water only, but it is not an alkali because it is insoluble in water.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Sodium hydroxide is soluble in water
Mark 3: Copper oxide is insoluble in water / does not dissolve

commonMistakes: Confusing compounds (chemically joined) with mixtures (not chemically joined)
Not recognizing that pure substances have fixed melting/boiling points while mixtures have variable ones
Mixing up when to use crystallization (obtaining solid) versus distillation (obtaining liquid)
examinerTips: Any question that wants the method to obtain (make) crystals, the process will be 'Crystalisation'
The constant temperature of distillation process = Boiling Point of pure liquid
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: pH scale diagram showing 0-14 with colours: red (0-3), orange (4-6), green (7), blue (8-11), purple (12-14). Label examples at each range.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (2 marks) — they asked students to identify which statement about acids and bases is always correct, testing understanding of fundamental definitions
N/D 2022 P1 Q8 (1 mark) — candidates had to determine which oxide reacts with both acids and alkalis, focusing on amphoteric oxide properties
M/J 2024 P2 Q5 (3 marks) — students were required to explain statements about weak acids, distinguishing between weak/strong and concentrated/dilute concepts
N/D 2023 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — tested enthalpy of neutralisation by asking about temperature changes when NaOH is added to different acids
M/J 2022 P2 Q7 (4 marks) — examined carboxylic acids homologous series and their acidic properties in organic chemistry context

workedExample.question: Explain why ethanoic acid has a higher pH than hydrochloric acid of the same concentration. Include an equation in your answer. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that ethanoic acid is a weak acid and hydrochloric acid is a strong acid
Step 2: Explain that HCl completely ionises: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻
Step 3: Explain that ethanoic acid partially ionises: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺
Step 4: State that fewer H⁺ ions means higher pH (less acidic)
workedExample.answer: Ethanoic acid is a weak acid while hydrochloric acid is a strong acid. HCl completely ionises in water: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻. Ethanoic acid only partially ionises: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺. Therefore, ethanoic acid produces fewer H⁺ ions in solution, resulting in a higher pH than HCl of the same concentration.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying weak vs strong acid, 1 mark for HCl equation, 1 mark for ethanoic acid equation with equilibrium arrow, 1 mark for linking fewer H⁺ ions to higher pH

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused concentration with strength of acids' — always distinguish between weak/strong (degree of ionisation) and concentrated/dilute (amount of solute) when discussing acid properties
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently wrote complete ionisation arrows for weak acids' — always use equilibrium arrows (⇌) for weak acids and bases, not complete reaction arrows (→)
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates lost marks by not specifying the medium' — always state 'in aqueous solution' or 'in water' when defining acids and bases using H⁺ and OH⁻ ions
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a pH scale from 0-14 showing: acidic region (0-6.9) in red, neutral point (7.0) in green, and alkaline region (7.1-14) in blue. Include common examples: battery acid (pH 0), lemon juice (pH 2), pure water (pH 7), baking soda (pH 9), household ammonia (pH 11), and sodium hydroxide (pH 14). Mark strong acids (pH 0-1), weak acids (pH 3-6), weak bases (pH 8-11), and strong bases (pH 13-14).
note_data.quickRecap: Compounds are elements chemically joined together while mixtures are substances not chemically joined. Pure substances have fixed properties while mixtures have variable properties. Different separation methods are used based on the type of mixture: filtration for insoluble solids, crystallization to obtain solids from solutions, distillation to obtain pure liquids, fractional distillation for miscible liquids, and chromatography for organic compounds.
